mnemonics.title: DECENTRALIZATION Checklist
mnemonics.content: D - Distributed Ledger, E - Economic Incentives, C - Consensus Mechanisms, E - Elimination of Single Points, N - Network Topology, T - Token Distribution, R - Resource Requirements, A - Authority Distribution, L - Ledger Replication, I - Incentive Alignment, Z - Zero Trust Model, A - Autonomous Validation, T - Transparent Governance, I - Interoperability, O - Open Participation, N - Node Diversity

acronyms.expansion: Delegated Proof of Stake - Token holders vote for delegates who validate transactions
acronyms.usage: Used in blockchains like EOS and Tron for high throughput

acronyms.expansion: Byzantine Fault Tolerant - System can function even if some nodes behave maliciously
acronyms.usage: Used in permissioned blockchains and some consensus protocols

acronyms.expansion: Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance - Consensus algorithm tolerating up to 33% malicious nodes
acronyms.usage: Used in Hyperledger Fabric for permissioned networks

acronyms.expansion: Distributed Hash Table - Decentralized lookup service for P2P networks
acronyms.usage: Used in IPFS and Bitcoin for peer discovery

acronyms.expansion: InterPlanetary File System - Distributed file storage system using content addressing
acronyms.usage: Used for decentralized data storage in blockchain applications

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a three-column comparison: PoW (energy) = mountains of computing power, PoS (stake) = voting based on token wealth, DPoS (delegation) = representative democracy with few elected validators
Picture a railway network with multiple parallel tracks (shards) - each track handles its own trains (transactions) independently, while a central scheduler coordinates overall operations

Think of a city: Full nodes are like city archives storing all historical records, Light nodes are like people carrying only recent important documents, requiring verification from archives when needed

analogies.analogy: Like gold mining - requires significant physical effort (computational work) to extract valuable resources, making it costly to attack the network
Like having a security deposit - validators put up money as collateral; honest behavior earns rewards, dishonest behavior loses the deposit
Like a potluck dinner - everyone brings a dish (data) and shares with everyone else; no single person provides all the food (no central server)
Like having multiple checkout lanes at a supermarket - instead of one long line, several parallel lines process customers faster
Like a shareholder meeting - token holders vote on important company decisions, with voting power proportional to shares owned
